The SEC disclosures today confirm that the SEC thought decentralization was the key difference in the risk profile of certain digital assets as compared to traditional securities, and that it was therefore the appropriate attribute to asses for purposes of Howey.

The disclosures also prove why calls for more guidance from the SEC are well founded (even the SEC is unsure how to define decentralization) and why, absent such guidance, congressional action is necessary.

@jesse_hynes Agreed, but no one is saying that web3 systems are legally entitled to avoid securities laws if they're decentralized.

@jesse_hynes Okay, but even if Hinman had never given that speech, the legal reasoning is still sound. Decentralization obviates many of the risks securities laws are meant to protect against, thus securities laws should not need to apply where true decentralization occurs.

The license that a protocol's code is published under is an important factor in the protocol's decentralization. 

It makes sense to use a restrictive license when a protocol is pre-token in order to guard against the protocol being forked/tokenized maliciously. 

Post-token launch, such protection is less necessary, but may nevertheless be important to fostering growth of the protocol's network. Where restrictive licenses are used post-token launch, they should not impede app-layer diversity (which could undermine the protocol's permissionlessness or censorship resistance) or be used to accrue value to the DevCo instead of the community.

Think the 2 biggest questions about Prometheum that remain unanswered are:

1. How will the listing of a digital asset on Prometheum be anything other than a death sentence for most projects?

2. How will they get around the disclosure requirements of Rule 144 or Rule 15c2-11?

On the first point, if a digital asset has a functional purpose but is deemed to be a security, then it's highly likely that its functional purpose will be negated. For instance, even if ETH had an issuer and that issuer agreed that ETH was a digital asset security and wanted to list on Prometheum, the recognition of ETH as a security would destroy the functionality of the Ethereum blockchain. The blockchain would no longer be able to issue ETH to validators and users wouldn't be able to expend ETH to pay fees, because now all of those transactions would be securities transactions. As a result, the Ethereum blockchain couldn't continue to function.

On the second point, it's theoretically possible that a combination of Rule 144 and Rule 15c2-11 could be used to trade "digital asset securities" on an ATS. But they can't facilitate such trades without the information required by those rules being made publicly available. That information includes audited financials and other information that isn't relevant to the price of a given digital asset and that no issuers make available. So how will Prometheum do it? Aaron regularly deflected questions on this subject, so I don't think they have a plan, but at the end of the episode he indicated that he thought on-chain data might actually be more useful for investors. 

So, my suspicion continues to be that there's another step to this parlor trick. If Gensler's back is against the wall (ie, if it looks likely that Congress will move forward with a market structure bill), he may provide extremely narrow No Action Letter relief regarding the disclosure requirements for "digital asset securities" under these rules. This would enable Prometheum to list a few digital assets and continue the illusion of there being a compliant pathway, which could dissuade Congress from acting on market structure. Meanwhile, the NAL would enable the SEC to maintain strict control over the listing of digital assets (a power Congress never intended them to have) and drive the industry to move outside the U.S.

@adamsternbach @BrianQuintenz Agreed that a different asset-based disclosure framework would be appropriate for many types of digital assets, but if the value of a digital asset is dependent on an issuer, then issuer-based disclosure and other protections are likely warranted. Otherwise, what's to protect consumers/retail investors from being dumped on by insiders who have asymmetric information? Ultimately, decentralization is what changes the risk profile. 

Approaching this from another angle. Suppose a biotech company tokenized revenue rights with respect to a to-be-developed drug. If the company still needed to guide that drug through clinical trials and FDA approval, then I think it's pretty hard to argue that issuer-based disclosure wouldn't also be necessary even though future revenue is going to be dependent on patient demand. If that wasn't the case, this pathway would be a fairly significant loophole to securities laws and it would change how the biotech industry currently finances drug development, with investors ultimately being exposed to significantly more risk.

@fewture @michaelselig Howey is and always has been a transaction-based test. You apply it to a transaction to determine whether securities laws should apply to said transaction. It's not an asset-characterization test that imparts permanent security-or-not status on an asset.

@michaelselig Why is this part bad for the SEC? 

Has the SEC ever actually included in a complaint a charge that the tokens themselves were ICs/securities? All of the complaints I'm familiar with allege that certain transactions were subject to securities laws, not that the tokens themselves were securities. 

Admittedly, they use sloppy language in a lot of their filings, but in the actual charges for securities violations, I don't think I've ever seen them say a given token was and always will be a security (even though that's generally what the industry thinks the SEC is says).

Further, the guidance and releases they put out in 2017-2019 make it pretty clear that the SEC thinks Howey is a transaction based test, not a tool for classifying whether a specific asset is a security. So, I'm not sure the SEC would even dispute that portion of the ruling in the Ripple case.

Might surprise a lot of people to learn that even the SEC doesn't think that ordinary tokens are securities. 

No SEC complaint has ever called for a judge to rule that an ordinary token was a security, and the SEC's own guidance dating back to 2018 (below) notes the distinction. 

Despite the noise on this topic, Howey is, and always will be, a transaction based test. An ordinary token sold in one transaction might be an investment contract or it might not be, but it never has been a security.

@NYcryptolawyer I don't think it's that simple. In both cases, there wouldn't be an investment contract if courts follow Supreme Court jurisprudence, as outlined in CB's brief.

But if courts reject the requirement that there be an ongoing contractual obligation, then in both cases you could have an investment contract in the primary sale if the issuer/promoter is promoting the asset as an investment opportunity (which is obviously unlikely in the case of concert tickets). Easy to say in those circumstances that the primary sale (whether anonymized or not) is a "scheme" (if that word is read more broadly than to only encompass enforceable contractual obligations).

Even if the primary sales are investment contracts though, subsequent secondary sales shouldn't be unless the primary/secondary sales are all part of a distribution scheme like what takes place in underwritten offerings.

The SEC didn't allege the existence of any scheme like that in the CB matter, so even if the court rejects CB's arguments, the court should still find that the transactions on CB's exchange aren't investment contracts, and should rule in favor of CB.

@david_r_barrera @a16zcrypto @paradigm @coinbase Thanks. I understand your view, but it doesn't get me over the line. Aren't all the cases you cite related to primary transactions? In my view, Howey is much better suited for primary transactions than secondary transactions. For primaries, it's easy to apply. For secondaries, it's really difficult.

Further, I don't think our argument only wins if the court believes there's a slippery slope.

Our argument is that the SEC's contortion of Howey to apply it to secondary transactions in the CB matter is so expansive that it has boundless potential to subject ordinary asset transactions to securities laws. It's not that this is just the first stepping stone to further expanding securities laws to cover additional transactions. With this case, the SEC is anointing itself with a new power - a tenuous expansion of Howey to give itself new vast authority.

As such, a finding that the secondary sales of tokens on CB were securities transactions would mandate in the name of justice that Tesla, used Tesla dealers, Taylor Swift, Ticketmaster and StubHub all be subjected to the same fate.

Just like the token issuers did, Tesla touts future functionality and utility for Teslas that could increase their value (and I don't know about the rest of the country, but in SF many people are buying them as investments) and Taylor hyped the epicness of her to-be-played concerts, driving a very active secondary market for them. Further, both could be said to indirectly benefit from the existence of secondary markets, because the mere existence of such markets makes people more comfortable participating in primary markets.

But neither Tesla nor Taylor marketed and sold Teslas or tickets as investment opportunities or as part of a scheme to generate secondary market activity in those assets. And the SEC similarly failed to allege sufficiently that the token issuers named in the complaint were doing that. It's also worth noting that the token issuers likely put in place 12 month minimum lockup periods on any private sales, thereby ensuring that token acquirers were not purchasing with a view to further distribute to the public (per Rule 144). Neither Tesla nor Taylor apply lockups to their primary sales. 

Ultimately, it'd be wrong result if Teslas or Taylor's concert tickets were deemed to be offered and sold as part of investment contracts. It'd similarly be wrong to find that secondary market transactions of the tokens in the CB complaint are investment contracts.

Furthermore, even if all of the above weren't true, the proper approach here would have been for the SEC to go after the token issuers themselves, and having established that they engaged in unregistered securities offerings, the SEC then could have brought actions against exchanges. Though, as we argued in the brief, the success of those cases (and this one) would depend on whether or not the original distribution and subsequent secondary transactions were all part of a scheme and that common enterprise existed among the issuer and the secondary purchasers.

But the SEC skipped that step because it's inconvenient and time consuming. Instead, it's acting arbitrarily and capriciously in order to target the chokepoints for an industry that it has verbally denigrated and clearly has a vendetta against. No where is this more evident than it's transparent attempts to treat good actors and bad actors as though they're the same.

The world has changed since Howey. Liquid secondary markets are now much more common for all types of assets, not just securities. If Congress wants the SEC to have jurisdiction over those markets, it should say so.  But the Supreme Court says that otherwise cannot be the default.

(1) You say: "The SEC is not applying Howey to secondary transactions at all. The SEC is only applying Howey to determine whether a token-based investment contract scheme was originally established and is ongoing."

Why wouldn't the complaint have been the proper place to explain why secondary market transactions in the assets were securities transactions? (3) The fact that all of those other asset transactions are in the "gray zone" is exactly the problem with the SEC's legal theory. 1/ Today, @BrianQuintenz and I published our analysis of 5 recent actions to answer some key questions:

🔹Is the SEC’s case against Coinbase’s wallet justified?
🔹Is the Uniswap class action result significant?
🔹Is the CFTC the industry’s new boogeyman?

Grades and summary 👇 https://t.co/4fDcYzpNBg

3/ Overall, the actions we analyzed are generally consistent with RANP’s focus on businesses rather than software - there is no conclusive evidence of “targeting developers” solely for developing, publishing, and deploying code.

4/ Nevertheless, both the CFTC and SEC actions received lower ratings because of their regulation-by-enforcement approach and their failure to foster innovation. 

Beyond that, the actions differ substantially with respect to their application of existing laws.

5/ The SEC's extension of broker rules under U.S. securities laws to the activity engaged in by Coinbase in relation to its wallet is both unnecessary and counterproductive – guidance and tailored rulemaking would do more to protect investors and foster financial innovation.

6/ The CFTC has demonstrated a more principled approach. 

The regulations that the CFTC uses clearly apply to the business activity challenged and their application was foreseeable. 

The actions, in our evaluation, do not violate notions of fairness and due process.

7/ But we strongly agree with Commissioner Mersinger’s dissent that the CFTC should have  brought these products/services into a sandbox that would foster innovation. 

The CFTC’s mandate to promote responsible innovation is being undermined by a lack of action.

8/ So:
🔹Is the SEC’s case against Coinbase’s wallet justified? ➡️ No.
🔹Is the Uniswap class action result significant? ➡️ Yes.
🔹Is the CFTC the industry’s new boogeyman? ➡️ No.

What are *positive control* and *negative control* of multisigs and why does it matter?

🔹"Positive control" is the ability to enact a change – the multisig can unilaterally pause a smart contract, change its parameters or upgrade it.

🔹"Negative control" is the ability to stop a change – the multisig can veto an action taken by tokenholders.

The two have different risk profiles and justify different regulatory treatment. More risk justifies more regulatory intervention.

There are a lot of additional ways to reduce risk (increasing signatories, delays, transparency, decentralized token ownership, etc.), but none are more significant than switching to a negative model from a positive model.

As a result, the entire sector would be better off if builders, investors and users pushed for multisigs to adopt negative control models.

We're entering a new era of decentralization.

As we keep seeing over and over again, when control of a powerful system or platform is in the hands of a few (let alone a single leader), it’s too easy to encroach on user freedoms. 

That’s why decentralization matters — It’s the tool that allows us to democratize systems by enabling credibly neutral, composable internet infrastructure; promoting competition and ecosystem diversity; and allowing users more choice, as well as more ownership.

But decentralization has been hard to achieve in practice — at scale — when pitted against the efficiency and stability of centralized systems. Meanwhile, most web3 governance models have involved DAOs (decentralized autonomous organizations) that use simplified yet burdensome models for governance based on direct democracy or corporate governance — which are not designed for the sociopolitical realities of decentralized governance. 

However, thanks to the “living laboratory” of web3 over the past few years, more best practices for decentralization have been emerging — These include:
🔹 models for decentralization that can accommodate applications with richer features; and 
🔹 methods such as DAOs embracing Machiavellian principles to design more effective decentralized governance that holds leadership accountable. 

As these models and methods evolve, we should soon see unprecedented levels of decentralized coordination, operational functionality, and innovations.

The SEC v. Coinbase faceoff today was the first critical test of the SEC’s enforcement strategy against digital asset exchanges. 

That strategy is more high-risk than the SEC’s historical focus on token issuers, so heres's some important background/context...👇

Before last year, the SEC’s approach had primarily focused on token issuers, with dozens of enforcement actions between 2017 to 2020 and a flurry of investigations launched during 2021 and 2022. But after the collapse of FTX in Nov 2022 and the SEC’s struggles to bring cases against issuers, the SEC appears to have shifted more of its attention to trading platforms, targeting Coinbase, Binance, and Kraken in 2023.

What’s driving the shift? Trading platforms are a critical chokepoint for decelerating blockchain-based innovation. They act as a gateway for new users, there are only a few servicing U.S. customers and they are easily found. Through their enforcement actions the SEC likely hopes to seize control of what tokens U.S. trading platforms can list.

Meanwhile, measuring decentralization of individual issuers/protocols under Howey is a labor-intensive process and there are thousands of them, so there’s no way to scale regulation-by-enforcement sufficiently to stem the tide of issuers. It’s an endless game of whack-a-mole.

Nevertheless, the application of Howey to secondary markets (like Coinbase) is unprecedented and is even more confusing than when it’s applied to token issuers. The SEC’s attempt to do so could be construed so broadly as to apply to any asset sale, including buying Taylor Swift tickets on Stub Hub.

As a result, the Coinbase, Binance and Kraken cases aren’t slam dunks for the SEC and they’re unlikely to produce clear rules of the road or provide significant consumer protection. Instead, inconsistent court rulings and long time horizons will likely only breed more uncertainty – we’ve already seen this from the divergent Ripple and Terraform rulings.

Ultimately, regulation-by-enforcement is no substitute for formal rulemaking and public comment processes, regardless of whether the target of that enforcement is a trading platform or a token issuer.

Thus, the SEC’s newfound focus on trading platforms raises the stakes while, ironically, making actual progress just as unlikely.

1/ In the last few days, we've gotten a great look at how the SEC's strategy against @coinbase and @binance is going to play out.

Here's how that strategy could be used to target decentralized exchanges (DEXs) next and why those attacks should fail. 🧵

10/ “No Action Letters” for platforms with similar functionalities (eg, Finders, Internet Portals, Online Bulletin Boards) are all consistent on this point – 

Where there is no potential for execution risks or conflicts of interest to arise, broker regulations should not apply.

11/ That means the extension of broker rules to Coinbase’s wallet is unwarranted and counterproductive – the risks the rules aim to shield people from are adeptly mitigated by the transparency of blockchains and smart contracts. 

So, broker rules shouldn't apply.

12/ The same reasoning applies to most DEXs, so SEC attacks on most DEXs using broker rules should fail.

That may not deter this SEC though, as its actions against decentralization are more about stifling innovation than delivering on its stated mission.

2/ The SEC's focus on centralized exchanges (CEXs) is a shift from its historic focus on token issuers.

The rationale for that shift (exchanges are a critical chokepoint and act as a gateway for new users) also applies to DEXs.
https://t.co/iIDlsBbZRV

3/ Fortunately, the SEC already conceded that DEXs aren’t “exchanges” under securities laws (one of the reasons why they’re seeking to expand the definition of “exchange”).

So, their best weapon against CEXs is likely off the table. 

But that may not deter them.

4/ The SEC’s primary allegation against DEXs will likely be that they are operating as “unregistered brokers”. 

This is similar to the allegations brought against the swap feature of Coinbase’s wallet, which functions like a DEX. 

So, is that claim against CB likely to succeed?

5/ Probably not. 

Even if we assume the SEC’s theory that the digital assets available for swapping via the wallet are subject to U.S. securities laws (the highly contested issue being argued in court), the SEC’s allegation that Coinbase is a broker should fail. 

Why?

6/ Per Coinbase, the wallet doesn't custody funds, negotiate or structure transactions, or make recommendations; it merely facilitates user-directed actions.

In fact, the wallet doesn’t implicate nearly any of the 16 factors commonly used by the SEC in finding broker activity. https://t.co/8H54n9wY9N

7/ The SEC’s claim ultimately hinges on the alleged 1% fee Coinbase charged on all wallet swaps. 

However, the SEC’s own policy rationale for applying broker regulations where there is transaction-based compensation does NOT support an action against Coinbase.

8/ In particular, the swap feature of the wallet does not give rise to any conflicts of interest that might be mitigated through regulation – 

There’s no “salesmen’s stake conflict” because there is no solicitation for investment or recommendation for purchases or sales.

9/ Yes, Coinbase makes more money from more sales, but without a specific solicitation for transactions, the conflict of interest is no different than what exists in ordinary business arrangements that never trigger broker regulations (e.g. sales of cars, art, homes, etc.).

This new paper proposes a compelling and novel solution for one of the most critical web3 regulatory challenges – illicit finance. 

Few thoughts on how this solution fits within the "Regulate Web3 Apps, Not Protocols" framework:

TL;DR: The proposal fits within RANP and is complementary to its approach, which is focused on the regulation of web3 businesses, not software.

Let's dive in. The proposed framework for tackling illicit finance is broken down into three parts:

🔹(1) Measuring Decentralization – The team proposes to first measure the decentralization of a given DeFi protocol (looking at whether any individual has independent control of the protocol) in order to determine whether traditional regulations applicable to TradFi the BSA might be applicable. This is a logical first step. 

We used the same concept to establish criteria for determining whether any regulation should be applied to on-chain activity. Ultimately, where activity is truly disintermediated, traditional intermediary-based regulation does not make sense and a different approach is warranted. Where that disintermediation is merely a facade, it may make sense to apply traditional regulation.

🔹(2) Establishing Certain Protocols as Critical Infrastructure – The team next proposes that certain well-established protocols be deemed to be "critical infrastructure" and subject to oversight by the OCCIP. The proposal for such oversight is not a proposal that would violate the autonomy of such protocols. Rather, it would seek to be used to enhance the security and resilience of such protocols and reduce their operational risk.

In our RANP series, we similarly proposed a sliding scale approach to applying regulation to applications, with more established apps having a heavier regulatory burden. Further, we considered that this framework would entail certain requirements with respect to the underlying protocols that such applications gave users access to, such as code audits and disclosures.

As a result, these concepts can collectively be used to benefit users without subjecting software to regulation, which would diminish their global utility. Further, these approaches do not unnecessarily hinder developers, as the sliding scale approach would leave developers of nascent technologies undisturbed by regulation.
 
🔹(3)   Critical Communication Transmitters (CCTs). Finally the team proposes to apply certain tailored financial integrity risk management obligations on certain service providers that facilitate communications, most notably node-as-a-service providers for RPCs.

This proposal addresses a shortfall in the RANP framework when it comes to illicit finance. 

In particular, by proposing to only apply regulation to applications, RANP would not account for illicit finance activities of users interacting with non-compliant apps or directly with smart contracts (in circumvention of regulated applications). This new proposal would capture such activity without applying compliance obligations to RPC operators that would necessitate subjective determinations they aren't capable of making. For example, as discussed in the paper, an RPC operator can easily engage in risk scoring of a wallet, but cannot easily determine whether or not an individual transaction involves a token that is a security.

While it's true that bad actors can also technically circumvent compliant RPC operators, regulation does not need to eliminate the possibility of illicit use to be effective. This is particularly the case where the elimination of illicit activity would require regulation that is so burdensome that it negates the benefits of the technology or stifles innovation of potentially beneficial technologies. The proposal strikes a nice balance in that regard.

Overall, this proposal is a noteworthy effort that demonstrates a very nuanced understanding of the risks and benefits of blockchain technology. It should therefore be a useful tool for policymakers and regulators.

Having now read the Coinbase ruling, here are my top four takeaways:

1⃣ Decentralization – The ruling is consistent with the guidance the SEC provided in 2018 and 2019 about decentralization, so to the extent token issuers have been following that guidance, not much should change. 

2⃣ Brokers – The reasoning for the dismissal of the claim against CB’s wallet tracks the argument we laid out in October on why that claim should fail. This is a big win for the industry and decentralized technology, but I am worried it will drive the SEC to accelerate their proposed rule change regarding the definition of “exchange” to scope in DEXs. More:
https://t.co/xpm4DIU6jw

3⃣Major Questions Doctrine – I'm surprised how one-sided the judge’s opinion is on MQD. In the Uniswap class action, the same judge indicated that Congress needs to determine how digital assets are regulated. Further, she’s completely neglected to contemplate the implications of the SEC’s legal theory. If an investment contract can exist where there are no rights to ongoing efforts, it’s very difficult to see how Taylor Swift tickets and Nikes aren’t investment contracts. That's far more impactful on the economy than what the judge assessed.

4⃣Staking as a Service – The court’s discussion of staking as a service is troubling as it sets a very low threshold for what constitutes “managerial and entrepreneurial efforts.” CB’s offering was appropriately designed to not involve much discretion and be very technical-only, so it’s hard to see how it involves managerial or entrepreneurial efforts. This could have significant implications more broadly for anyone relying on decentralization under Howey.

@CloutedMind @eawosikaa @a16zcrypto @VitalikButerin I very much agree with this as well. 

My running thesis is that capital will seek out opportunities regardless of whether or not they're productive, and that the current market trend towards unproductive is the result of a combination of there being (1) a lack of productive use cases and (2) distrust of productive use cases.

One could argue that responsibility for both of these issues rests with any number of different market participants (investors, entrepreneurs, influencers, etc.). However, it's pretty clear to me that this is driven by the fact that the market is oriented towards short-term incentives. I think that's the natural orientation for improperly regulated markets, so I think ultimate responsibility here rests with the SEC.

If there were clear rules of the road that permitted companies to build decentralized protocols and there was consistent enforcement against projects, then the orientation of the market could be turned towards the long-term as it is comparably in equities.

As it currently stands, bad actors take advantage of the lack of clarity, and the lack of swift enforcement means that outsiders have difficulty telling the difference between good actors and bad actors.  So, now we are where we are.

Few thoughts:

1. DeFi/Derivatives - On your first and last post, I agree the bill doesn't really address the CFTC's authority with respect to crypto derivatives products. I don't think it expands it, but it does mostly leave the current state of play in place. That's not ideal given the spate of enforcement. 

The expansion of the CFTC's authority appears to be mostly focused on digital commodity spot markets, and I don't read that authority to subject DEXs to the same requirements as centralized exchanges, which I think is a win. Do you agree?

Obviously, it'd be great if there was a pathway for digital commodity derivatives, particularly those offered by DeFi platforms, as the disintermediation and programmatic functioning of DeFi solves a lot of the risks the CEA is intended to address when it comes to derivatives, but isn't that kind of an entirely separate regime/focus? I'm not sure I'd condition improving our SEC situation on also getting clarity for derivatives, though I think reasonable minds can disagree and I think a bill that protects all DeFi would be great.

2. Decentralized Governance System - On your second post, I can't say I know what a "commodity pool operator" even is, but my read of this is that they're carving out DAOs of DEXs as opposed to DAOs like Ooki that were operating a frontend that was violating the CEA because it facilitated derivatives trading. This goes to the same point above. I don't think the DAO of a DEX would be scoped into the regime merely for administering and maintaining the affairs of a DEX. Right?

3. Tokens vs. Securities - On your third post, I 100% agree that the definition of digital assets is narrowly tailored, meaning that the bill really only provides a pathway out of securities laws for tokens that don't look like traditional securities, but that the SEC has tried to subject to securities laws under Howey. Think that's the majority of assets in the space though. Also agree that it'd be better to get regulatory clarity for things like stablecoins and digital assets that might be notes or profit participation interests. 

Seems like it'd be easy enough to scope those in, but maybe they'd need a different test than the "decentralized network" and "functional network" tests? 

I also think there's a major concern about not creating massive loopholes to securities laws, where any financial instrument can escape security status just because it's digitized. It's hard for me to argue with that, as otherwise the bill would be the end of securities laws (which I appreciate some people may prefer, but I don't think is realistic).

@ryandavidwill @a16zcrypto Thanks for the feedback. Think there's a bit of nuance on the legal side that missing from the article.

While there are some that believe that protocol fee accrual to tokens creates risk under securities laws because it satisfies the "profit" prong of Howey, I personally think that risk is overstated. The SEC has always taken the position that if a token has the potential to appreciate, then that is sufficient to satisfy the profit prong. This is also why the SEC has claimed that it doesn't matter whether a token has "utility" if it can appreciate, ultimately the "profit" prong is satisfied. Thus far, courts have generally agreed with that argument.

However, there is case law beyond Howey that raises questions about whether combining voting rights and economic rights creates issues under securities laws. Importantly though, that risk could be mitigated where tokenholders don't have voting control of the economics. That also potentially mitigates some risk associated with protocol fees and DAO control.

That's why we've suggested that protocols use governance minimization where possible, and where it isn't, they should consider the DUNA. We believe both of those strategies could enable the fee models without increasing risks under securities laws.

@p0Gnosis What you're proposing isn't necessarily inconsistent with what I've stated above. 

Governance tokens can give tokenholders access to a treasury to direct future spending efforts on behalf of the protocol. 

They can also provide control of intellectual property that enables tokenholders to restrict use of a protocol's IP by third parties.

But "protocol equity" falls far short of "equity". Tokens don't entitle holders to ongoing efforts of any third party, nor do they entitle holders to direct those efforts. 

Further, tokens are permissionless. The DAO of Token A doesn't vote on whether or not Token A can be used as collateral in a lending protocol, as an asset in a bridge or as an asset in an index token. 

Anyone can build anything that gives a token more utility and/or increases its value. That doesn't happen with equity.

Tokens aren't equity.

They shouldn't entitle you to direct the ongoing efforts of a centralized team.

They shouldn't entitle you to restrict how builders build with them.

Instead, they should enable permissionless building and value creation. 

Anyone can build anything.

@Jenny_10_05 @david_r_barrera So, even though a person finances Tesla's operations by buying a car from them, what you're saying is that you don't think it's reasonable for that person to expect to profit from investing in a Tesla such that the transaction should be subject to securities laws, even though Elon Musk actively touts that the development and release of full-self driving is going to massively increase the value of that car?

https://t.co/IKDokQ5HaL

@david_r_barrera I generally agree with you when it comes to primary transactions. An issuer selling an asset while saying its going to expend efforts to make the price of that asset increase looks like a securities offering. In such cases, it is very easy to establish that statements by an issuer gave rise to a reasonable expectation of profits. And I think the entitlement in that case is clear – the issuer made representations to the buyer to induce them to make an investment – regardless of whether there's an actual contract.

But applying Howey to secondary transactions tortures the legal reasoning that underpins the case and extends the reach of securities laws to ordinary commerce. In secondary transactions, the buyer is less likely to have been the recipient of representations by the issuer and we should therefore be very skeptical of a finding that the buyer was "reasonable" in their expectation of profits based on the efforts of the issuer.

Courts haven't really wrestled with the consequences of what constitutes a reasonable expectation in the context of secondary transactions, but if it turns out that innocuous statements to the public are sufficient to establish that expectation, then the SEC will be empowered to regulate a huge swath of the economy. 

That's not to say that Howey can't apply to secondary transactions. For instance, we wrote in this brief about how an underwriting scheme to distribute to the public shouldn't be a backdoor to escaping Howey's clear applicability to primary transactions. https://t.co/2r6epmMKsZ

But there needs to be a limiting principle. Without one, sales of Teslas are securities transactions:

1. Investment of money ✅ – You purchase the car.
2. Common Enterprise ✅ – Tesla holds cars as inventory and can produce more.
3. Reasonable Expectation of Profits Based On the Efforts of Others ✅ – Elon has explicitly stated that once they launch FSD, the value of Teslas will increase dramatically. https://t.co/IKDokQ5HaL

@david_r_barrera The point is that under solicitation laws, you wouldn't have any claim against a party for making statements relating to an asset unless you were purchasing that asset from that party. Investors aren't reasonable in relying upon or acting on innocuous statements by third parties. Howey should be similarly constrained.

Regarding Tesla, that's not what I'm saying. I don't think primary or secondary sales of Teslas are securities transactions. But if one takes the position that secondary sales of SOL are securities transactions, there is no intellectually honest way to conclude that secondary sales of Teslas are not securities transactions. Sure, you could try to argue that people are generally buying Teslas to use them, but there is ample evidence that many people are investing in Teslas because they think they'll be able to profit by renting them out as part of a carshare service once FSD is available.

@david_r_barrera Yes. And this wouldn't be a novel approach. If you look at how solicitation claims are treated under U.S. securities laws, there's a lot of case law that constrains what types of statements constitute a solicitation.

A solicitation claim requires plausible allegations that the defendant successfully solicited the plaintiff's purchase, which generally requires that the defendant (1) urges the plaintiff to buy the relevant security, (2) in a manner that causes the sale (3) with a motive to benefit himself or the security's owner.

The same type of public statements the SEC argues create a "reasonable expectation of profit" would never meet the threshold for "urging the plaintiff to buy the relevant security" under a solicitation theory.

Importantly, solicitation claims are also constrained to instances where the plaintiff purchases publicly offered securities from an issuer, its controlling shareholders or underwriters. It doesn't apply to secondary market transactions more broadly. That is an implicit acknowledgement that general public statements do not have or should not have the same effect in the context of secondary transactions as they do in primary transactions.

This is important. Exporting the uncertainty and ambiguity of U.S. securities laws to the entire world by expanding their extraterritorial reach in violation of Supreme Court precedent is a huge mistake.

@JoelKatz @CoinDesk Okay, so Howey applies to primary transactions, as there’s always a contractual relationship between the issuer and buyer in a primary sale. @JoelKatz @CoinDesk So, having failed to articulate what the universal limiting principle of Howey could be that wouldn't blow a hole in securities laws, you now attack a limiting principle for a specific asset class because that's not a "technologically neutral" approach?

I'd be more than happy to see the SEC's ability to weaponize Howey against other asset classes constrained. But the argument that any approach has to treat all asset classes the same way is illogical.

Different asset classes present different risks to their holders. Transactions in gold are not regulated the same way as transactions in securities because the risk profile of those assets are different. 

Blockchain technology's functional characteristics (namely, that it enables uncontrolled, transparent and trustless computational networks possible) can be used to mitigate the risks that securities laws are intended to address, thereby making the risk profile of their underlying digital assets function more like gold and less like a security. @JoelKatz @CoinDesk Sure, but if a blockchain network is unilaterally controlled by a party, then its underlying digital asset faces similar risks as securities borne out of trust in the team that holds that unilateral control. 

Conflicts of interest, rent seeking and any number of dishonest actions can be taken by the controlling party to extract value to the detriment of the digital asset holder. Those actions don't necessarily give rise to fraud, so relying on anti-fraud statutes to protect the digital asset holder from the controlling party aren't sufficient.

Per Howey, an investment contract is a contract, transaction or scheme where there's an investment of money in a common enterprise with a reasonable expectation of profits based on the efforts of others. 

If the Supreme Court had intended to limit that test to contractual relationships, it would have done so. And if by "scheme" it had meant "contractual relationship" it wouldn't have included that word when "contract" is already present.

Obviously, that's very squishy and opens up the statute to weaponization for political purposes, which is what we've seen the current SEC do. 1/ Centralized control of networks has consequence — Big Tech, Big Banks, and Big AI control public discourse, financial access, and even information. 

Decentralization eliminates control, but it needs to be incentivized. Here's how. 👇

2/ Decentralization isn’t easy.

Centralized systems are efficient, coordinated, and fast-moving. They’ve dominated.

By comparison, decentralization is inefficient and comes with immense costs.

Centralization is like gravity, and it’s hard to overcome the natural order.

3/ Blockchain technology is a rocketship though — it can help us escape gravity, at scale.

Many have already demonstrated this — anyone can own, act, and build permissionlessly, without gatekeepers.

But even though it's now possible, decentralization isn’t inevitable.

4/ Incentives matter.

The costs of decentralization are extreme — it requires giving up control, diluting ownership, and inviting competition.

Without proper incentives, fake decentralization thrives while true decentralization struggles.

5/ Regulation is how we can and should level the playing field — rewarding those that reduce risk by giving up control, while holding centralized systems using blockchain technology to merely obfuscate control accountable.

@val_usa_crypto @jchervinsky The SEC's framework was a 1-pronged approach -- decentralization to eliminate all information asymmetries. This hasn't worked.

A 2-pronged approach is better -- (1) decentralization to eliminate information asymmetries arising from control,  (2) disclosure reqs to eliminate information asymmetries arising from ongoing efforts.

The SEC’s decentralization framework has been corrupted and used to persecute builders —incentivizing rug pulls over value creation.

It’s time to fix it.

Decentralization should mean the absence of control, not the suppression of ongoing efforts.

Here’s how and why👇

Network tokens — tokens that derive substantial value from the operation of a blockchain (eg BTC, ETH, SOL) — can have embedded trust dependencies that result in information asymmetries. 

Where those trust dependencies are minimized, network tokens look like commodities with low information asymmetry risk. 

Where those trust dependencies are significant, network tokens can look like securities with high information asymmetry risk — supporting the use of disclosures requirements under securities laws.

The two primary trust dependencies relating to network tokens are: (1) control and (2) ongoing efforts.
The SEC’s 2019 Decentralization Framework sought to eliminate the risk of information asymmetries by defining decentralization to mean the absence of BOTH control and ongoing efforts. Their reasoning was clear — where network tokens were “sufficiently decentralized” tokens functioned more like commodities.

But this is the wrong approach! 

It’s created significant perverse incentives — builders can actually reduce their regulatory risk by abandoning their projects or by masking their ongoing efforts (“decentralization theater”). 

Builders HATE this conception of decentralization.

We need to incentivize builders, not hinder them. A better two-pronged approach can achieve this:

1⃣Decentralization should be redefined as “the absence of control.” This eliminates significant risk and preserves one of the core features of blockchains – they can function without human control. 

2⃣A targeted and light touch disclosure framework can be used to eliminate information asymmetries that might arise from ongoing efforts.

Collectively, this approach would foster innovation and protect users – ensuring decentralization empowers builders rather than being weaponized against them.

@DavidRhodus Non-memecoins are legal. You just have to contend with Howey.

The critical takeaway here isn't about memecoins. 

It's that the SEC's interpretation of Howey continues to be that an investment contract may exist based on the "economic reality" of an arrangement.

Why does that matter? 

The SEC's recent optimistic approach to the industry and the dismissal of many of its cases has led a lot of people to believe that "everything is legal". 

That's particularly true because most of the cases the SEC has been dropping/settling involved arguments by the defendants that, rather than "economic reality," Howey's investment contract analysis hinged on whether there was a formal contract. 

So, if the dismissals were a sign that the SEC was accepting of this "predicate contract" argument (and abandoning the "economic reality" argument), nearly every token in existence would be legal. 

This release confirms the SEC doesn't believe "everything is legal" and that's a good thing. Plus, their reasonable approach to memecoins is further confirmation that the SEC wants to take a much more progressive and constructive approach to crypto. That's cause for celebration.

It was an honor to join the @SECgov roundtable on crypto regulation yesterday. I commend @HesterPeirce and the Crypto Task Force for convening the meeting and all the other speakers for participating.

The picture that emerged for me yesterday is that there is (1) broad agreement that the SEC should  regulate primary sales (like ICOs) as securities offerings, and (2) general consensus that a moderate principles-based approach should be used for secondary sales (like transactions happening on Coinbase).

I'm incredibly optimistic about this vision and what it means for the industry's future. Critically, this consensus sits between two extremes:
🔹One extreme wants to subject all sales of tokens to securities laws simply because people are investing.
🔹The other extreme wants to exclude all sales of tokens from securities laws simply because there isn’t a legal relationship between the issuer and investor.

At first glance, the simplicity of these approaches may seem intuitive. But both approaches prioritize form over substance, which fundamentally distorts the function of securities laws. So, in the simplicity hides potential catastrophe.

With respect to the first extreme, securities laws do not apply merely because the form of a transaction is an "investment". If they did, the SEC would be empowered to control the entire U.S. economy, inserting itself whenever someone buys a home, a diamond ring, or a baseball card. 

With respect to the second extreme, securities laws can apply to transactions even where no legal relationship exists between the issuer and investor — what matters is substance (economic reality), not form (a legal relationship). Artificially separating the token from its inherent risks ignores reality and assumes that the transaction happens in a vacuum. This is illogical for three reasons:
1⃣ People invest in tokens without the existence of a legal relationship;
2⃣ Information asymmetries with respect to a token can arise without the existence of a legal relationship; and
3⃣ An issuer can unilaterally control the risk associated with a token without the existence of a legal relationship with the investor.

As a result, the second extreme creates a form over substance regulatory loophole that leaves investors exposed to significant harms—the very types of harms securities laws were intended to address. Further, this approach would incentivize the propagation of synthetic securities—issuers promising economic benefits that replicate securities, but evading securities laws because those promises aren’t embedded in the asset. 

As evidence, this approach would treat ETH (whose value is solely derived from a decentralized network) the same as FTT (whose value was solely derived from a centralized company, FTX). And if a form over substance approach can’t distinguish between ETH and FTT, it also can’t distinguish those assets from "Apple App Store Token" or "Pfizer Vaccine Token". So, not only would this approach threaten the crypto industry, it would undermine the entire framework of securities laws.

Given the foregoing, I firmly believe we must move away from the extremes and towards a moderate principles-based approach that prioritizes substance over form. We recently submitted a comprehensive  paper to the Task Force on how that can be accomplished using a control-based decentralization framework, and we look forward to sharpening that vision with our portcos and the community more broadly.

The @SECGov just released excellent guidance on stablecoins — clearly identifying where they fall outside the scope of securities laws.

While the guidance mostly focuses on centrally-issued stablecoins, there are some implications for decentralized stablecoins. In particular:

🔹The SEC Howey and Reves analysis can easily apply to decentralized stablecoins, meaning that where decentralized stablecoins aren't marketed as investments and otherwise meet the criteria outlined in the guidance, they should similarly fall outside the bounds of securities laws.

🔹The SEC's emphasis on the potential for interest payments to turn stablecoins from a consumer product into an investment product under both the Howey and Reves tests makes sense, particularly in the context of centrally-issued and controlled stablecoins. 

🔹That doesn't mean decentralized stablecoins that pay interest are automatically securities though. Most decentralized stablecoins that pay interest do not depend upon the "entrepreneurial or managerial efforts of others" to generate the yield to pay interest — everything is handled via decentralized smart contracts that aren't controlled by anyone. As such, they do not satisfy Howey.

🔹Decentralized stablecoins that pay interest also shouldn't satisfy Reves where there is no issuer "raising money for the general use of a business enterprise or to finance substantial investments" and where reserves are "adequately funded to fully satisfy redemptions on demand". Further, a lack of control over the smart contracts and reserve that underpin the stablecoin (i.e., "decentralization") substantially reduces the risks associated with the stablecoin.  

As a result, I read the guidance as leaving open the possibility that the SEC believes truly decentralized stablecoins don't trigger securities laws, even where they pay interest.

Finally, the guidance is broadly consistent with the GENIUS and STABLE bills moving through Congress — both bills prohibit payment of interest to ensure stablecoins are not investment products, which justifies the exclusion from securities laws for stablecoins included in both bills.

Calling for an end to crypto’s offshoring sparked some debate. Consolidating some questions and responses here:  

1️⃣ What about tax? 

Offshore foundations can offer tax benefits, but there’s more risk here than many people think (and more than advisors are willing to admit). Plus, to access those benefits, U.S. projects have to introduce significant operational complexity and structural inefficiencies—like finding offshore employees and maintaining strict independence of the foundation from the devco, among other things. Every minute spent managing these constraints is a minute not spent shipping.

Most founders I talk to would, in retrospect, gladly give up the tax efficiencies they got from offshoring to eliminate the overhead that comes with those structures. They often say they never would have used those structures if had purely been about tax as finding product-market fit is far more important than optimizing for tax. 

Further, properly structured correctly, the DUNA can be very tax efficient.

2️⃣ Aren’t Public Benefit Corporations (PBCs) and Foundations similar?

No. PBCs maintain fiduciary obligations to shareholders, but they’re allowed to balance those with public benefit. This means they can operate like an ordinary company—compete, raise capital, pursue profit—including where those businesses provide ancillary benefits to the protocol and tokenholders. 

Foundations distort incentives and are at a competitive disadvantage. Even where they can engage in commercial activity, they’re rarely effective. And when they try to behave like companies, they risk triggering the very tax, legal, and governance contradictions they were set up to avoid. 

There’s a reason there are only a few successful consumer internet products and services built and operated by foundations. 

3️⃣ Is there a role for foundations going forward? 

Definitely. The main point of my piece wasn’t “no foundations ever”—it was that market structure legislation should eliminate their use for decentralization theater. That’s a good thing.  

Foundations (particularly domestic) with focused mandates—like executing grant programs and coordinating ecosystem-wide efforts—will continue to be useful. Tokenholders can provide oversight, but are not well-positioned to manage these functions directly. Uniswap Foundation is a good example. It operates independently, but is accountable to tokenholders through funding—if tokenholders don’t like the foundation’s decisions, they can defund it. For mature projects, foundations may also be a better place to house protocol-development work, as we’ve seen with the Ethereum Foundation. 

Importantly, the proposed control-based framework doesn’t just help DevCos—it also legitimizes these targeted, purpose-driven foundations.

To conclude:
•If you're running grants, use a foundation.
•If you're building a product, use a company.

@panekkkk @DKerr_Cowrie The answers to all your questions are in the Clarity Act. Under that framework, tokens are not required to relate to mature networks when they’re initially issued or start trading on secondary markets. But they do have to be network tokens and can’t be profits interests in companies (like FTT) or foundations. 

From there, the bill provides a flexible pathway to progressively decentralize (relinquish control) over time, with minimal obligations prior to maturity (mainly disclosure). It provides clear guidelines around what constitutes control, with appropriate allowances for delegations of authority to decentralized governance. 

Is it perfect? No. Is it directionally right? Yes. 

But I’m all ears if you have a cogent legal framework that would work better without being incongruent with securities laws.

@panekkkk @xjehmz @DKerr_Cowrie Yeah, it's complex. It's definitely improved from FIT21, but still complicated. 

I think of it as two gates: (1) qualifying as a digital commodity, which gets you crowdfunding, airdrops and secondary market trading, and (2) qualifying for the network maturity concept, which enables insiders greater secondary market access. 

The vast majority of projects qualify for Gate #1 right out the gate. There are very few that look like FTT that wouldn't qualify. And then Gate #2 is going to depend on the project and its facts and circumstances. I like that maturity is a principles test with one specific rules-based safe harbor.  That provides both certainty and flexibility.

On your specific questions, I think they relate to Gate #2:
 - A company controlling a specific instance of a frontend wouldn't make a difference as anyone can operate a frontend. So operating one version doesn't give you control of the protocol.
- Pause functionality is similarly permitted under the "impartial system" maturity criteria.

Agree with you that the bill leaves a lot of things open to interpretation, but I think that will mostly be addressed during rulemaking. That's one of the reasons it's important to get a law passed now, while we actually have regulators that want to solve the challenge constructively.

@sh0edog Very much agree with you that there's a path for foundations with focused missions (like grant programs) and that those types of foundations can be a boon to credible neutrality. And where foundations are targeted, the non-profit element isn't problematic. I wrote about that in a follow up piece (below).

However, most foundations aren't currently set up that way. Instead, they're used as a shortcut to decentralization, with many projects looking more like foundation-led tokenized businesses than decentralized networks/protocols. Compounding the issue, most are set up as offshore vehicles with corporate directors who don't understand anything about the technology or ecosystem, yet wield significant amounts of control. In this context, they are extremely extractive and obfuscate decentralization.

On DUNAs, yes they are non-profits. But you shouldn't conflate them with foundations. DUNAs are by definition extremely targeted organizations — they are literally just a wrapper of token governance. They are not a hierarchal tool for building, marketing, or other operations. So, as is the case with grant-focused foundations, the non-profit status doesn't impede their operation. 

But non-profit status is extremely problematic when you're trying to build an actual business. Most networks/protocols need businesses built on top of them. They need GTM efforts, engineering efforts and marketing efforts all working in sync to attract third-party businesses. They need to build tools and ancillary products. Non-profits are not well suited for this activity. And non-profits headed by foreign lawyers in an offshore jurisdictions have no hope. 

There’s a reason why there are only a few successful consumer internet products and services built and operated by foundations.

You're right that greater reliance on DevCos could subvert decentralization. However, that doesn't necessarily need to be the case. Proper incentive alignment between a network/protocol and a DevCo does not necessitate that the DevCo have control. DUNA and BORG tooling as well as the other mechanisms discussed in my piece can help ensure that.

@daranda Less mature projects tend to have more volatile valuations and significant sales of the token can cause bigger price swings. That's a dangerous combination for a treasury management company.

Few questions nagging me about treasury management companies:

(1) How do you comply with ongoing PubCo disclosure obligations? If you disclose ongoing efforts of the digital asset issuer, you're increasing securities laws risk for the digital asset. If you don't, you may be committing fraud.

(2) How do you reconcile a commitment to digital asset holders to support the price of the asset with equity capital raises of the PubCo with the Gary Plastic case?

(3) What do you do when the shares eventually trade below NAV?

These problems are manageable for treasury management companies being set up by third parties for very mature projects like BTC, ETH, and SOL. I'm not sure these problems are solvable where management teams of less mature projects are spinning up and controlling these public vehicles themselves.

Crypto lawyers should be doing a better job here.

@david_r_barrera Hmm, I agree with you on what their focus is, but I disagree with you that this somehow means they’ve abandoned a substantive test. 

They’ve long acknowledged that the asset itself isn’t a security, it’s the transaction of the asset that may be subject to securities laws as a result of the economic reality surrounding the transaction. 

You can disagree about whether that’s the right approach (I think it is), but if you accept that as the case then their approach in these statements makes sense. A person providing staking related services with respect to an asset that doesn’t involve that person engaging in managerial or entrepreneurial efforts shouldn’t be deemed to be a securities transaction as the asset itself isn’t a security and the act of staking doesn’t creat an investment contract with the service provider.

But that’s completely separate from the analysis that applies to transactions of the underlying token, which may be subject to securities laws given the economic reality of the asset. 

I think that’s the right result. A SaaS provider shouldn’t be deemed to have engaged in a separate securities offering when they offer staking services for a blockchain just because that blockchain doesn’t meet the SEC’s threshold of decentralization. To hold them accountable would require you to have determined that the asset itself is a security, which is at odds with their theory of the law.

Further, it would make no sense for them to look at staking services through the economic reality lens but not apply the same standard to transactions in the underlying asset.

(3) As to the liquid staking token itself, in the example you give, I don't think the below statement would be true.

(4) And finally, with respect to the language you've quoted from the Protocol Staking Activities statement, that footnote means that, as a threshold issue, the tokens eligible for the guidance are those that don't have those properties that make them implicitly enumerated securities. Overall, I think if you look through all of the SEC's releases, including the launch of "Project Crypto" you'll see consistent use of the "economic reality" concept, which is a clear indicator that the SEC continues to believe substance trumps form. Formalistic approaches are the opposite of that.

It just needs the law to recognize facts:
🔹Developers aren’t money transmitters—they don’t control user funds.
🔹DeFi protocols aren’t intermediaries—they don’t custody assets or effects trades.
🔹Network tokens aren’t securities—no one controls their value, supply, or functionality.

That recognition does not require special exemptions, but it does require legislation to reconcile outdated legal frameworks with a technology that removes the very risks those laws were meant to mitigate—custodial risk, discretionary control, and information asymmetry.

If we get that clarity, crypto wins — all property becomes cryptography (h/t @balajis). 

But if we push for special treatment where it is not warranted—for actors who do control funds, do effect transactions, or do sell synthetic equity interests in business activities—we not only lose the principles underpinning the industry, we invite the scams, frauds, and blowups that could be its undoing.  

Crypto wins by being better, not by negating rules that still matter.

@ChainLinkGod Definitely nuanced. 

I don't know what the META token would entitle holders to. If it's a network token, then it should entitle holders to some value accrual from a functioning protocol or network (maybe swaps?). I don't think that conflicts with value accrual to Consensys' equity value accrual. Conversely, if it entitles holders to some revenue resulting from Consensys' ongoing operations, then it'd be a company-backed token, making it more appropriately regulated like a profits interest and bringing it into direct conflict with Consensys' equity, whose value is also depends on Consensys' ongoing operations.

Generally, I'm in favor of turning network/protocol fees on wherever possible. But even when fees aren't turned on, if the system is properly designed, the network effects of the system should accrue to tokenholders, not to equityholders. Tokenholders, not equityholders, should control the protocol.

I also fundamentally believe that companies shouldn't be precluded from building on top of networks/protocols and earning their own revenue. That's not a requirement placed on any other builder within those systems, so applying it to the original team is a tacit admission that the infrastructure isn't credibly neutral and treats the team as an indentured servant of the protocol. Further, that approach will be net negative to the protocol, as it means the team that understands the protocol the best isn't economically incentivized to build out distribution on top of it (they would be the sole bearer of the cost of that buildout, but would socialize the benefits if the value only accrues at the protocol).

Nevertheless, conflicts of interest should be minimized wherever possible. That wasn't really doable under the prior administration, where incentive alignment increased risks under securities laws. That was obviously dumb and bad for tokenholders. Market structure legislation would resolve that.

That risk exists in traditional equity markets as well though, where companies are materially reliant on single source suppliers, for instance. 

I also don't think token-only models eliminate the conflicts of interest risk, they often just pretend it's not there. For example, if a token project is heavily dependent on the ongoing efforts of a foundation, that foundation has substantial leverage to extract more value from the network, and just because it doesn't have shareholders doesn't mean it won't exploit that leverage. @hasufl People are welcome to pursue any model they want. 

Personally, I don't think token-only structures are superior to token-equity structures. I think DevCos can and should operate value accruing businesses that don't have anything to do with the token, but that may result in ancillary benefits to it. Nor do I think token-only models eliminate conflicts of interest in any meaningful way, it's mostly just optics. 

As an example, if Base were to launch a token that derived its value from sequencer revenue from transactions on Base, would that mean that Coinbase has to shut down and get rid of its equity? Would it mean that Coinbase shouldn't operate its exchange, even though it directs some portion of users to DeFi deployed on Base, thereby benefitting the the hypothetical Base token? Or would it mean Coinbase has to operate its exchange for free, even though that wouldn't economically be possible and likely wouldn't benefit Base?

Approaching it from another angle, if you applied crypto's regulatory distortions and immediate secondary market liquidity to any other startup industry (AI, gaming, consumer internet, etc.), I think you'd end up with a similar result to what we've seen in non-financial crypto: hype/extraction triumphing over longterm innovation.

@hosseeb Too early might be the better argument, and I appreciate why that's hard to disprove. But I don't think your framing of the impact of regulation is correct. It can't be so easily discounted.

No jurisdiction in the world has sought to regulate blockchain technology by embracing it's core strength—open decentralized networks that can operate independently of any company.   

Instead, they've all sought to just regulate blockchain technology like other technologies, like products developed by a company. None of those regulatory regimes have driven projects/products that actually deliver on blockchain tech's benefits. None of them have helped, or have helped projects, crystalize the value proposition of blockchain tech for consumers.   

That doesn't mean specific non-financial use case like web3 gaming will work if we get the regulatory scheme right. The incumbents' grasp on consumer attention is extreme.  But assuming non-financial use cases won't ever work is assuming that people aren't ever going to care about owning their digital lives. That assumption is heavily influenced by recency bias, and, as I've argued, that recent period was severely distorted by a regulatory scheme that fostered the worst possible conditions for innovation (a lack of transparency and long term incentive alignment).

People wildly underestimate how damaging bad regulation is to innovation.

The lack of clear rules in crypto didn't just slow progress—it fundamentally distorted what got built, who benefited, and how markets functioned.

Specifically:

1⃣Anti-Transparency—The weaponization of US securities laws meant that transparency became a liability. Builders were told to avoid speaking openly and plainly about their systems, economics, or roadmaps. They were told to avoid marketing altogether.

How can startups succeed when they can’t talk about what they’re building?

The forced opacity in crypto undermined trust, slowed adoption, complicated partnerships, and prevented the ecosystem from maturing as other tech sectors do.

Innovation doesn’t thrive in the dark.

2⃣Adverse Selection—In an enforcement-by-ambiguity regime, trust in the rule of law diminishes. The result is the creation of powerful short term incentives for value extraction.

Careful, good-faith builders moved slowly and deliberately, trying not to cross any lines, but would still get targeted by regulators. Meanwhile, profiteers moved quickly to capture attention, capital, and users, and then would disappear before the regulators knew what happened.

Naturally, products were optimized for greed and product market fit became an afterthought. That's unsustainable for any industry, not just crypto.

When regulations incentivize bad outcomes over good, that’s exactly what happens.

3⃣Structural Contortions—When no one is playing by the same rules, the lowest common denominator becomes the default.

Shortcuts to decentralization, dubious Cayman Island foundation structures, token distributions optimized for legal superstition, and governance systems excluding the people actually building all became the norm.

These contortions eroded all of crypto’s strengths—openness, decentralization, and shared ownership—and made the alignment of incentives impossible.

Innovation doesn’t happen without incentive alignment.

4⃣Inefficient Markets—Capital efficiency and rational pricing don't emerge organically in distorted markets.

Crypto might be the largest experiment ever to demonstrate that without a baseline regulatory framework, markets drift towards vibes over fundamentals. They become less rational, not more.

The disastrous U.S. regulatory approach is what drove cycles of memecoins and nihilism instead of innovation.

Markets need rules.
————
Given the above, what’s striking, is not that crypto hasn’t yet delivered on expectations. What’s striking is that it made it to this point, where it sits on the precipice of overcoming all of these distortions.

The GENIUS Act has already legitimized dollars onchain. Project Crypto at the SEC and CFTC are legitimizing securities, derivatives, and other assets onchain. And the CLARITY Act will legitimize the building of the blockchain networks that underpin all of these and other use cases.

With CLARITY, builders can speak plainly. Economic models can be pursued. Roadmaps can be published. Governance can include builders. And guardrails against profiteers, rugpulling, and self-dealing become the norm.

Innovation can happen without distortion.

CLARITY doesn’t guarantee success. But it does create the world’s first regulatory framework for building open networks rather than companies—It creates a legal architecture that finally matches crypto’s technical architecture.

Once that happens, crypto can finally use its strengths at scale. In that world, crypto's opportunity looks a lot more obvious.

Legacy market infrastructure is slow, expensive, and inaccessible. Moving onchain solves these problems. 

But capturing that opportunity depends on clear rules for the blockchain apps that enable investors to trade peer-to-peer, without rent-seeking middlemen. The @a16zcrypto and @fund_defi safe harbor proposal for DeFi apps would accomplish exactly that. 

Today, former SEC Chief Economist and Director of the Division of Economic and Risk Analysis Craig Lewis submitted an economic analysis of that proposal to the Commission.

Though scoped to the proposal itself, Lewis' analysis speaks to something larger: the economic costs and benefits of tokenized securities broadly, and what blockchain technology could mean for the future of financial markets.

His bottom line: there are real costs, but the overall economic case is compelling and the proposal offers a sound path forward.

We just responded to the @CFTC on their proposed rulemaking for prediction markets.

Prediction markets are one of the most powerful tools we have for turning dispersed knowledge into actionable information. Unlike polls, they're continuous and incentivized. Participants put money behind their beliefs, so prices reflect genuine conviction, not casual opinion.

These markets are growing fast. Kalshi's average weekly volume surged tenfold this year to $3 billion. But the bigger story is what comes next. AI agents can process vast information and execute trades autonomously on permissionless blockchains. Together, these technologies point toward something much bigger than what exists today.

That said, regulatory uncertainty threatens that future.

Our solutions would help unlock it—reaffirming the CFTC's exclusive jurisdiction, strengthening contract resolution, limiting manipulation and insider trading, adopting a case-by-case approach to public interest determinations, and exploring alternative pathways for compliance.

Prediction markets aren't just about placing bets on events. They're information machines. The rules the CFTC writes now will determine whether this technology is constrained in its infancy or allowed to reach its potential.

We hope the Commission seizes the opportunity.

On Friday, @a16zcrypto filed a response with the @USOCC on their GENIUS Act rulemaking to implement the historic U.S. stablecoin legislation.

Here’s some of the key issues we address, and why they matter:

1️⃣ The OCC’s “one issuer, one brand” model adds cost and compliance burden without commensurate benefits. Brand-specific reserve attestations and clear account titling, recordkeeping, and segregation for reserve assets can help achieve the same safety outcomes at far lower cost.

2️⃣ The OCC’s approach on yield is over-broad and ineffective, sweeping in common business arrangements that GENIUS didn’t intend to capture. Issuers shouldn’t be responsible for what they can’t control. The rule should stop there.

3️⃣ Congress excluded decentralized networks and their underlying software from GENIUS’ framework. But the OCC’s approach creates uncertainty and a risk of inconsistent application by regulators. The OCC should clearly carve out DeFi activities consistent with the CLARITY Act to protect developers and ensure harmonization.

4️⃣ Decentralized stablecoins are deliberately carved out of GENIUS—they aren’t issued by a person and are entirely disintermediated, so they don’t pose the same risks as centralized payment stablecoins. To make this explicitly clear, the OCC should reaffirm that decentralized stablecoins are outside the framework.
—
As @cdixon put it, stablecoins are money’s “WhatsApp moment.” More than $12 trillion in value moved through them last year, and we expect volumes will continue to increase as builders keep improving the global finance stack. 

The GENIUS Act will unleash this future and demonstrates what’s possible when effective policy and technology work together. 

Now let’s get the implementation right.

Today @a16zcrypto filed our response to the U.S. Treasury's GENIUS Act NPRM—one of the first federal efforts to implement U.S. stablecoin legislation.

This one sets the standard for how state regimes can comply with the federal framework. 

Here’s why that matters:
1⃣ State regimes need to align with GENIUS to promote stablecoin fungibility. 
Stablecoins only work for payments if they’re fungible. A stablecoin issued in one state must carry the same value as one issued federally or anywhere else. Materially different state frameworks with different reserve, redemption, capital, liquidity, or risk management procedures break that—and recreate the fragmented, multi-state patchwork that has burdened payment networks for decades. 

Making all key GENIUS requirements uniform preserves fungibility and provides a path for growing stablecoin companies to transition from state to federal oversight—unlocking the true benefits GENIUS was intended to provide.

2⃣ GENIUS' definitions need to be adopted to preserve the core protections that the crypto industry fought for. 
States shouldn't get to reinterpret terms like “payment stablecoin” or “digital asset service provider.” Congress reserved specific areas for states—licensing, chartering, and supervision—and provided for uniformity elsewhere. Uniform definitions preserve GENIUS’ important protections for decentralized stablecoins and DeFi innovation.

3⃣ Passporting rules need to be clear. 
A host state shouldn’t be able to impose its own requirements on an issuer already licensed elsewhere. Certified state regimes should passport across state lines—that’s what a level playing field looks like, and that’s how we get more innovation in internet-native money.

As @cdixon put it, stablecoins are money's “WhatsApp moment.” More than $12 trillion moved through them last year, and volumes will keep climbing as builders improve the global finance stack.

The GENIUS Act demonstrates what’s possible when effective policy and technology work together. Now let's get the implementation right.
