Adopting the Creative Commons Zero (cc0) license gives everyone the right to adapt and build upon IP (without fear of legal consequences). 

This jumpstarts meme-ability by actively inviting the creation of derivative works. https://t.co/Op1F4neC50

All of that said, cc0 isn't a one-size fits all solution – the right IP model depends on your strategy and goals. Brands that are already well-established, for example, might opt for more restrictive licenses as a way of protecting their existing IP and reinforcing exclusivity.

And cc0 licensing reduces friction that might otherwise stop brand-reinforcing extensions from being created – or worse, lead to them in a way that bypasses the original, as @tshirtbob_ recently explained in the context of his cc0 @acommonplacenft: https://t.co/SBNh8T8Hd5

As a business professor, I'd be remiss if I didn't mention business models:

Because cc0 projects typically earn royalties from secondary sales, third-party expansions and derivatives can also become sources of revenue, as they drive increased demand for the original cc0 assets.

As the NFT space continues to evolve, we expect to see more infrastructure and design patterns supporting cc0 efforts, as well as innovation around value capture. It's also likely we'll see creators develop completely novel rights&licensing models. 

QED. https://t.co/gX17e8JLbZ

But for some projects, at least, cc0 licensing is an incredibly compelling way to kickstart IP that may one day take on a life of its own.

Intrigued? Full article is here: https://t.co/gX17e8JLbZ

But it's not enough to simply build cc0 and assume "they will come" – derivatives don't magically appear! There are factors that help here: https://t.co/gX17e8JLbZ

As with open-source software, cc0 NFTs work best where there is potential for a rich, expanded ecosystem.

Examples: Loot bags are basic primitives that make sense for all kinds of adventure settings; the Nouns brand is as intuitive for a beer ad as for physical glasses; and the @goblintown art style looks good on everything from @Dirt_Birds_NFT to @valkilmer. https://t.co/X9CvGYgzHe

For NFT projects that are trying to build novel brands, these sorts of derivatives can be a win-win for all. 

Derivatives borrow some brand awareness from the source project; their creation and sharing can later drive new interest in the original.

If you see someone wearing @nounsvision glasses on the street (or in a Super Bowl commercial), you might want to get a pair of your own – but you also might become interested in buying an original @nounsdao NFT or some other related derivative.

Just as smart contracts are composable "building blocks" that can be stacked and remixed to create richer applications, NFTs and their underlying smart contracts can act as a base-layer foundation for culture and creativity to recombine and interconnect. https://t.co/57ES1ZSCer

This can work particularly well with NFTs because ownership enables people to internalize the results of the co-creation through the value that accrues to their assets and contributions – and that in turn incentivizes them to participate in co-creation in the first place.

cc0 allows this to happen with the explicit permission of the original creators – thus giving an NFT's enthusiast community a *literal license to build* new value layers whenever they want – 

"transform[ing] an adversarial game into a co-operative one" https://t.co/kWLBDNvGUe

@0xfoobar I think the point is that we might want to give up some efficiency of allocation in exchange for providing a more direct reward to creators. This isn't an issue in contexts like with most fungible tokens where the spread is just a pure tax that doesn't have a welfare weight.
@0xfoobar We might both want to reward creators for pure social welfare reasons and because doing so provides more incentive/opportunity for them to invest in creating.

@0xfoobar @akbarpour_ ... what really matters is how much the structure of the market makes it possible to target redistribution through market behavior. We make that point a bit more precise in this follow-up paper: https://t.co/RaLyD4gscn
@0xfoobar @akbarpour_ And then the question is just why should we put a high social welfare weight on creators? ...

@0xfoobar @akbarpour_ Effecting this transfer through the market is actually very effective from a social welfare perspective because we transfer more on the margin to the creators whose work generates more high value transactions.

@0xfoobar @akbarpour_ If we know that creators are a category of people we place a high social welfare weight on, and that weight is high enough (which it might be, for reasons to be discussed below), we might be happy to trade off some efficiency in exchange for effecting a transfer to creators.

@0xfoobar @akbarpour_ If you're confident that a particular category of individual has high welfare weights relative to the shadow value of revenue, then you want redistribution towards them; you also might want redistribution if market behavior directly reveals inequality in the welfare weights.

@0xfoobar @akbarpour_ Just for reference, the latter category is for example when market behavior reveals inequality -- e.g. if somebody has low willingness to pay for an important medical treatment, we infer they have a high shadow value of money, rather than that they don't want the treatment.

@0xfoobar @akbarpour_ So in that sense the royalty might be more efficient then, say, a big lump sum subsidy to all creators -- the royalty targets the rewards to the creators who generated the most value.

@0xfoobar @akbarpour_ So the royalty question doesn't map directly to the paper because the paper is only considering the buyer and seller, whereas the creator is a third party BUT I think the basic logic is the same:

@0xfoobar @akbarpour_ The first category is the one I think is most relevant for the debate about creator royalties -- that's an easily identifiable category (even identifiable on-chain!) that we might directly want to reward from a social welfare perspective.

@0xfoobar @akbarpour_ We might do that because we think they're a deserving category from a social perspective and/OR because they have positive externalities and/OR because boosting their welfare in the market generates more of an incentive for people to become creators in the first place.
@0xfoobar @akbarpour_ We show that in general you sometimes want redistribution, but not always, depending on the structure of the inequality/welfare weights in the market...

In a new #EconomicTheory paper, @J_W_Hatfield and I show that vampire attacks can result in virtuous platform competition that drives down prices. https://t.co/jMsbkZjEmA

In a “vampire attack,” a platform tries to directly incentivize its competitors’ customers to switch.

This isn’t a new idea (airlines have had “status match” for ages), but it’s much easier in the world of crypto, where every transaction is stored publicly on a blockchain.

In short: Vampire attacks restore competition for loyal customers. https://t.co/jMsbkZjEmA

This theory broadly accords with what we’ve seen among NFT trading platforms in recent weeks, with vampire attacks leading to intense competition for customer loyalty.

Moreover, as @BernsteinShai and I discussed in @HarvardBiz, the competition induced by vampire attacks can only benefit consumers if it doesn’t drive firms to engage in malbehavior (such as with centralized crypto finance platforms taking on excess risk). https://t.co/miq4Tsw1mO

A “wave mint” leverages people’s on-chain data and reputation to select for holders who are well matched to a project's culture and goals – while at the same time driving attention to the project, and helping avoid gas wars and bots. 🌊

An NFT mint largely determines who your community is, and how they and the broader market view the project. 

The question is, then: How can a new NFT project recruit holders who share the project’s values and goals?  

Read on! 👇👀 https://t.co/lahyVG4KqK
But from an operations perspective, a wave mint is complex to execute. And there are security issues around having people mint for wallets that all other, potentially high-value digital assets.

From a mechanism design perspective, the wave mint format means that people's wallets effectively function as a form of “proof of unique personhood.” And opening mint windows spontaneously and for short time periods makes the overall mint process harder to game.

Intellectual property is one of the largest and most important markets in the world – but it's also rife with market failures.

Defining ownership over ideas is fundamentally difficult, and this has led to complex and high-friction processes for managing partnering, licensing, and building around IP. The tension has become even greater with the rise of generative AI tools that accelerate IP creation and remixing tremendously, but have struggled with attribution and permissions.

These sorts of IP market design challenges have fascinated me since I first learned about them back in grad school. And in the decade and a half since, I don't think I've ever seen an infrastructure innovation that excites me as much as @StoryProtocol.

Story proposes a programmable IP layer for the Internet, linked to physical-world IP regimes through on-chain contracting and licensing modules – and open to third-party development and innovation. Building IP infrastructure is a perfect use case for the public, decentralized architecture blockchains provide – and I'm hoping it can help take both nascent and established IPs up to 11.

It's been a tremendous honor to collaborate with the Story team through my work at @a16zcrypto, and I'm thrilled that we're leading their Series B round, QED.

1⃣ With marketplaces and protocols alike, the optimal strategy is often to start broad, then narrow, and then broaden again. Starting broad gives you the space to find product-market fit — identifying the users who have the strongest demand for the category of transaction you enable (measured in terms of value creation). But then once you find those early adopters, focusing on them lets you optimize the business model, after which you can scale by expanding scope to adjacent use cases.

2⃣ Web 2 platforms and web3 protocols leverage network effects in different ways. Platforms have historically benefited from creating "walled gardens" to lock in their advantage over competitors. Protocols, by contrast, establish competitive advantage by becoming embedded in as many applications as possible — and that means they should explicitly support third-party development.

3⃣ With token design, monetary policy evolving over time should be the default. Owing in part to the Bitcoin precedent, many projects try to fix their entire tokenomic design (e.g., token supply, emission schedule, and so forth) up front. But that's rarely the right path. Not only is it really hard to fully calibrate an economic system before you've seen it in use, but in practice token supply and the associated design parameters should generally evolve along with the project ecosystem, as new uses and users change the dynamics of money demand.

Among other things, blockchains make it possible to create and record ownership of digital assets such as event tickets, ebooks, or personal identifiers without having to rely on a centralized platform like Ticketmaster or Amazon. Moreover, because these digital assets are embedded in software, publicly verifiable, and interoperable by design, they enable both their original creators and third parties to build value on top of that ownership in a very flexible way.

For a simple example, think about event tickets: it's actually really hard to engage in a secondary sale of a digital ticket when that ticket is manifested as a QR code in an email, because there's nothing to stop a person from "selling" the QR code to five different people (and so unless you really trust the seller, you wouldn't want to engage in trade at all). By contrast, if the digital ticket is encoded as an NFT on a public blockchain, then only one user account can own it at a time, and the exchange of the ticket for payment can be executed in a single unified software process -- each half of the exchange occurs if and only if the other half does, and the ownership of the ticket transfers in a way that means the buyer can be certain they now control it.

And then, because the digital ticket exists on a public web architecture, both the event host and third parties can add value onto it in a way the tracks with loyalty or rewards programs. For example, a local news website could give a discount to anyone holding a ticket from a local team's home game -- and they can do this without having to interface directly with the team or any third party database; they just enable customers to verify that they own the relevant NFTicket.

All of this happens without any intermediary like Ticketmaster or Eventbrite in the middle, and without the need for the event organizer to manage their own database or smartphone app. Plus, the digital ticket can survive beyond the lifetime of the event and even the event host itself, in the same way that physical tickets do. As @SteveKBark and I wrote in The Everything Token, this enables a variety of new models of digital identity and brand building.

Another example I give frequently is the idea of individually-owned digital assets to prove people's identity to social media and other platforms in a world of AI fakes. If you think about a driver's license, one of its primary advantages is that it's "forwards compatible" with new use cases that emerge over time -- when a new restaurant opens, for example, you can use the same driver's license you've always carried to prove your age; you don't need to get a new one specially configured for the restaurant. But most tech platforms don't work that way -- they have relatively closed APIs, and they, rather than the user, control which applications can access a given account's data. Blockchains make it possible to create individually-owned digital identifiers, sort of like a digital driver's license: the user holds an identity asset in an account they control and can use flexibly across different platforms, on an open tech standard. So in principle, you would only only need to get authenticated once, and then could use that single authentication asset to prove your identity to any website -- even those that come to exist in the future.

Framing decentralization in the language of control (or rather, lack thereof) is a fundamentally economic-theory way to define the concept. It's how I always explain it when teaching people how to reason about protocol incentive design, and it's how regulators should reason about it, too.

One of the most common questions I get about marketplace business is when should you start taking fees?

I work through how to reason about this in a new article for @a16zcrypto; here's a summary in haiku:

Contrary to what some believe, a "free market" is *not* a free-for-all. 

Well-functioning markets need rules – and crypto is no different. Without basic structure and protections, uncertainty and inefficiency will drive away legitimate businesses and consumers.

In @ftopinion today, I argue that crypto regulation needs to achieve four specific objectives:

1⃣ Predictability and stability: crypto needs clear, enforceable rules that businesses, investors, and consumers can understand and trust. 

2⃣ Protection of property rights: legal frameworks should reinforce and complement blockchain-based ownership.

3⃣ Transparency and information clarity: disclosures should help prospective crypto purchasers and investors make informed decisions.

4⃣ Fair competition: crypto needs guardrails against monopolistic practices, market manipulation, and fraud. 

The proper crypto regulatory framework also has to be congruent with existing rules, so as to avoid inadvertently creating workarounds to established protections. And it must recognize that crypto is more than just finance: it's tech infrastructure, too.

But all of this is possible – and the crypto industry must welcome it.

One of the most powerful yet least well-understood token types is the arcade token. 👾🪙👀

The name comes from the idea of an arcade where you swap cash for physical tokens you can use to play Tekken  Gator Panic – but the idea encompasses all manner of tokens-as-medium-of-exchange, including reward points and in-game currency.

Like with other token models, arcade tokens promote network effects and brand loyalty (there's a reason airlines have frequent flyer programs!).

But critically, arcade tokens exist in infinite supply and support an exchange economy by design. They are freely available from the issuer at a public "faucet price" and redeemable for a variety of goods or services. This economic model enables builders to print and provision value within their ecosystem, while keeping the token stable so that users can easily reason about what they're worth and when to redeem them.

This inverts a common (incorrect) assumption about tokens: neither fixed supply nor speculation are necessarily required for creating value. Arcade tokens aren't the right model for every business, but they're perfect for spend-centric economies, as well as blockchain-based reputation and reward programs.

@milesjennings, @eddylazzarin, @Tim_Roughgarden, and I have been helping teams think through arcade token designs for years at this point, and today we released an @a16zcrypto long-form to share our learnings on the model's opportunities, design trade-offs, and regulatory outlook.

As we gear up for the #SuperBowl this weekend (and the #Olympics starting today), eyes aren't just on the athletes – they're also watching the prediction markets. 📈🏈📉👀

And with all the attention, there's debate about what prediction markets are – are they trading? gambling? some sort of crowdsourced journalism tool? 

My answer as an economist – in @ProSyn – is simple: 

Prediction markets are *markets*, and markets are a fundamental tool for allocating resources and aggregating information. They introduce an asset that pays off if a given outcome occurs, which enables people to trade based on their beliefs about what will happen.

By contrast, a traditional sportsbook isn't trying to predict who will win, but to "balance the action" by adjusting the odds to attract bets on whichever side has less money behind it at a given moment. Vegas wants to make people willing to bet on long shots, whereas a prediction market enables people to trade based on what they truly believe.

Relative to other methods of forecasting, prediction markets have many benefits:

🔔 they give a probability estimate, rather than just an opinion share;

⏱️ the update in real time;

🪙 they're incentivized; and

🧑‍⚖️🏦🧑‍🏫🏀🦸 they cover a wide range of questions beyond what ordinary equities and commodities markets track.

But before prediction markets can fulfill their promise, they need: 

🏗️ infrastructure – ways to validate whether a given event has occurred, and ensure transparency;

💬 participation – people with all different types of relevant information must show up; and

🚫🕵️ protection against manipulation – we need to make sure people don't manipulate real-world outcomes to profit in prediction markets, and conversely that they don't manipulate prediction markets to try to impact outcomes.

QED 🏈

@mlstrat All of that said, I completely agree on the overall point you're making re: prediction markets! 

Weather insurance is a specific form of highly orthogonalized insurance product -- and prediction markets at least in principle offer a way to extend that idea to lots of other micro events people might want to insure against but not otherwise have access to an orthogonalized instrument for. (h/t @HermanNarula who first introduced me to this idea)

@mlstrat It's kind of the opposite, right? This has been a standard insurance category for a long time, and prediction markets are just providing a new somewhat decentralized mechanism for implementing it
